title: Brick Calculator per m² 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Bricks Per Square Meter: Wall Calculator
description: Calculate bricks needed per m² of wall. Standard 6×12×24 brick = 67 units/m² with common bond. Free and instant.
intro: This calculator tells you exactly how many bricks you need to cover a wall of any size. It uses the standard formula: divide the total wall area (width × height in m²) by the face area of a single brick, then add a waste factor. For a standard US modular brick (190 mm × 57 mm face, laid in running bond with a 10 mm mortar joint), there are approximately **48–50 bricks per m²**. The exact count shifts with brick size, bond pattern, and mortar joint thickness. Use this tool before ordering materials to avoid costly shortfalls or expensive overstock on your masonry project.
keyTakeaway: **Bricks per m² = 1 m² ÷ [(brick length + joint) × (brick height + joint)]** — for a standard US modular brick (190×57 mm) with a 10 mm joint: 1 ÷ (0.200 × 0.067) ≈ **74.6 → round up to 75 bricks/m²** before waste.
useCases: Estimating total brick quantity and material cost before starting a new residential garden wall or privacy fence.
Planning brick orders for a house facade or exterior cladding project to avoid mid-build supply delays.
Comparing how many bricks are needed when switching between standard modular, queen, or jumbo brick sizes.
Calculating leftover bricks after a renovation to decide whether to order a full extra pallet or source loose bricks.
Checking contractor quotes by independently verifying the brick count stated in a masonry bid.
explanation:
## How It Is Calculated

The core formula counts how many brick faces fit into one square meter, accounting for the mortar joint around each brick:

```
Bricks per m² = 1 / [(L + J) × (H + J)]

Where:
  L = brick length (m)
  H = brick height / course height (m)
  J = mortar joint thickness (m)

Total bricks = Wall Area × Bricks per m² × Waste Factor
Wall Area    = Wall Width (m) × Wall Height (m)
Waste Factor = 1.05  (5% standard) to 1.10 (10% for complex patterns)
```

**Example — standard US modular brick, 10 mm joint, single-wythe wall:**
- L = 0.190 m, H = 0.057 m, J = 0.010 m
- Bricks per m² = 1 ÷ (0.200 × 0.067) = 1 ÷ 0.01340 ≈ **74.6 → 75 bricks/m²**
- Wall = 4 m wide × 2.5 m tall = 10 m²
- Total = 10 × 75 × 1.05 = **788 bricks** (order 800 to round to full packs)

> **Note on wythe thickness:** The formula above gives bricks for a *single-wythe* (one brick thick) wall. For a double-wythe wall, multiply by 2; for a full-brick wall (one brick deep ≈ 90 mm), multiply by 2 as well.

---

## Reference Table

| Brick Type | Nominal Size (L × H × W) | Joint (mm) | Bricks per m² (single wythe) |
|---|---|---|---|
| US Modular | 194 × 57 × 92 mm | 10 | **75** |
| US Standard (non-modular) | 203 × 54 × 95 mm | 10 | **72** |
| US Queen | 203 × 70 × 76 mm | 10 | **58** |
| US Jumbo Modular | 194 × 70 × 92 mm | 10 | **59** |
| US Norman | 286 × 57 × 92 mm | 10 | **51** |
| US Roman | 286 × 44 × 92 mm | 10 | **65** |
| UK Standard (metric) | 215 × 65 × 102.5 mm | 10 | **60** |
| Australian Standard | 230 × 76 × 110 mm | 10 | **50** |

*Sources: Brick Industry Association (BIA) Technical Notes; ASTM C216 standard dimensions.*

---

## Typical Cases

### Case 1 — Garden privacy wall (US modular brick)
- Dimensions: 6 m wide × 1.8 m tall = **10.8 m²**
- Brick: US Modular → 75 bricks/m²
- Gross count: 10.8 × 75 = 810
- With 5% waste: 810 × 1.05 = **851 bricks** → order **900** (nearest full pack of 50)

### Case 2 — House facade cladding (US Norman brick, running bond)
- Dimensions: 12 m wide × 3 m tall, minus 2 windows (1.2 × 1.4 m each) = 36 − 3.36 = **32.64 m²**
- Brick: US Norman → 51 bricks/m²
- Gross count: 32.64 × 51 = 1,665
- With 7% waste (complex cuts around windows): 1,665 × 1.07 = **1,782 bricks**

### Case 3 — Double-wythe retaining wall (US Standard brick)
- Dimensions: 3 m wide × 0.9 m tall, double-wythe = 2.7 m² × 2 wythes = **5.4 m² equivalent**
- Brick: US Standard → 72 bricks/m²
- Gross: 5.4 × 72 = 389
- With 10% waste (corners + cuts): 389 × 1.10 = **428 bricks**

---

## Common Errors

1. **Forgetting mortar joints** — Calculating 1 ÷ (L × H) without adding the joint gives counts 15–20% too high (you'd order far too few bricks). Always add the joint width to both dimensions.

2. **Skipping the waste factor** — Even straight walls produce breakage and miscuts. A minimum 5% waste allowance is industry standard; corners, arches, and window reveals need 7–10%.

3. **Ignoring openings (doors & windows)** — Measuring the full wall rectangle and forgetting to subtract door and window areas inflates brick count and cost unnecessarily. Deduct each opening area before multiplying.

4. **Mixing nominal and actual dimensions** — ASTM nominal sizes *include* the mortar joint. If you apply a joint on top of the nominal size you double-count the joint and underestimate bricks per m² by ~5–8%.

5. **Assuming a single wythe for all walls** — Structural exterior walls in the US are commonly double-wythe or cavity wall construction, doubling the brick count. Confirm the wall specification before ordering.

6. **Not accounting for bond pattern** — Stack bond vs. running bond uses the same brick count per m², but herringbone or diagonal patterns generate 15–20% more waste due to angled cuts at edges.

---

## Related Calculators

- Area Calculator (m²)

faq.q: How many bricks do I need per square meter for a standard US modular brick?
faq.a: For a US modular brick (nominal 194 × 57 mm face) with a standard 10 mm mortar joint, you need approximately **75 bricks per m²** (single wythe). This is derived from 1 ÷ (0.204 × 0.067) ≈ 73.1, rounded up with a 5% waste factor to ~75–79 bricks ordered per m².

faq.q: What waste factor should I add to my brick estimate?
faq.a: The Brick Industry Association (BIA) recommends a **5% waste allowance** for straightforward walls with few cuts, **7–8%** for walls with window and door openings, and **10–15%** for complex bond patterns like herringbone or diagonal. Never order the exact calculated amount — brick breakage during transport and cutting is unavoidable.

faq.q: Does the bond pattern (running bond vs. stack bond) change the number of bricks needed?
faq.a: The *theoretical* bricks per m² is the same for running bond and stack bond since each brick occupies the same face area. However, patterns like herringbone or 45° diagonal require significantly more cutting at wall edges and corners, increasing effective waste to **15–20%** above a straight running bond. Always increase your waste factor for decorative patterns.

faq.q: How do I account for doors and windows in my brick count?
faq.a: Calculate the total wall area (width × height), then **subtract the area of each opening** (door width × height, window width × height) before multiplying by bricks per m². For example, a 10 m² wall with two 1.2 × 1.0 m windows has a net masonry area of 10 − 2.4 = 7.6 m². Still apply a 7–8% waste factor for the extra cuts around opening edges.

faq.q: What is the difference between a single-wythe and double-wythe wall, and how does it affect brick count?
faq.a: A **single-wythe** wall is one brick thick (roughly 90–100 mm deep) — common for garden walls, fences, and cladding. A **double-wythe** wall is two bricks thick (~200 mm) and is used for structural exterior walls in residential construction. Simply multiply the single-wythe count by 2 for a double-wythe wall, as both faces require a full layer of bricks.

faq.q: What ASTM standard governs brick dimensions in the United States?
faq.a: **ASTM C216** (Standard Specification for Facing Brick) and **ASTM C62** (Building Brick) govern the dimensional and physical requirements for clay brick in the US. Nominal modular brick dimensions are also codified in ASTM C652. These standards define the basis for the standard brick sizes used in this calculator.

faq.q: How many bricks come on a standard pallet, and how does that help my order?
faq.a: A standard US pallet holds approximately **500–525 modular bricks**, though this varies by manufacturer and brick size. Once you calculate your total brick need (with waste), **round up to the nearest full pallet** to simplify delivery and reduce per-brick cost. Ordering partial pallets typically costs 10–20% more per unit from most suppliers.

faq.q: Does mortar joint thickness really matter? What if I use a thinner joint?
faq.a: Yes — mortar joint thickness has a measurable impact. A **6 mm joint** instead of a 10 mm joint increases bricks per m² from ~75 to ~83 for US modular brick (an 11% increase). Conversely, a **12 mm joint** drops the count to ~72. Always confirm the specified joint thickness with your mason before placing a material order.

sources.name: Brick Industry Association – Technical Notes on Brick Construction (BIA TN 10)
ASTM International – ASTM C216: Standard Specification for Facing Brick
NIST – Metric System and Construction Units Reference

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, no external data updates required
